name: Falkland Islands
tagline: A wind-scoured South Atlantic archipelago — the Falkland Islands to the United Kingdom, Islas Malvinas to Argentina — whose contested sovereignty, a long pastoral colony, a 1982 war and an overwhelming 2013 referendum have made it one of the world's most closely watched disputed territories.
summary: The Falkland Islands (Spanish: Islas Malvinas) are a British Overseas Territory of some 3,500 people in the South Atlantic, about 480 km east of southern Argentina, which maintains a long-standing claim to the archipelago it calls the Islas Malvinas. Uninhabited at the time of their first recorded sighting, the islands saw competing French, British and Spanish settlements in the 1760s, and after Spanish administration from Buenos Aires and a post-independence Argentine presence, Britain re-established control in 1833 — the start of the continuous administration that defines the islands' present status. A pastoral colony built on sheep and wool grew up around a British-descended population, while the United Nations listed the territory among its Non-Self-Governing Territories and recognised a sovereignty dispute between Argentina and the United Kingdom. Argentina's 1982 invasion triggered a 74-day war that Britain won at the cost of roughly a thousand lives on both sides; in a 2013 referendum islanders voted almost unanimously to remain a British Overseas Territory, a result the United Kingdom upholds and Argentina rejects.

founding.label: Re-establishment of continuous British administration
founding.detail: The defining political-status moment of the Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas) is the British re-establishment of control on 2 January 1833, the start of the continuous administration that has governed the islands ever since. After a brief Argentine presence on East Falkland in the wake of Buenos Aires's independence from Spain — itself disrupted by a U.S. naval raid in 1831 — a British force arrived and required the small remaining Argentine garrison to leave, by Britain's and the islanders' own accounts without loss of life. A permanent British administration followed within about a year, and a civil lieutenant governor was appointed in 1841/1842. The United Kingdom dates its modern sovereignty and settlement from this point and bases its position on the wishes of the inhabitants and continuous administration since 1833. Argentina rejects this account, treating 1833 not as a founding but as the illegitimate occupation of territory it had inherited from Spain, and it has maintained a sovereignty claim ever since — a dispute the United Nations formally recognises and asks the two governments to settle through negotiation. This entry presents both positions without endorsing either.

quickFacts.label: Status
quickFacts.value: British Overseas Territory (UK); claimed by Argentina as the Islas Malvinas

quickFacts.label: UN status
quickFacts.value: On the UN list of Non-Self-Governing Territories; sovereignty dispute recognised under GA Resolution 2065 (1965)

quickFacts.label: Economy
quickFacts.value: Fisheries licensing (squid) and sheep/wool, with offshore oil prospects

eras.title: Empty Shores and Competing Claims
eras.standfirst: Uninhabited when Europeans first recorded them, the islands drew rival 18th-century settlements — French, British and Spanish — whose overlapping claims planted the seeds of a dispute that endures.
body: The Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas) had no indigenous population. The first widely recorded sighting is attributed to the English navigator John Davis in 1592, and Captain John Strong made an early landing in 1690, naming Falkland Sound after Viscount Falkland; the Spanish-language name Malvinas derives from the French Malouines, after sailors from Saint-Malo. For more than a century the remote, treeless archipelago in the cold South Atlantic remained essentially unoccupied, visited by sealers and whalers but settled by no power.

Settlement came in the 1760s, and with it the rival claims that still divide the islands. In 1764 the French navigator Louis-Antoine de Bougainville founded the first settlement, Port Louis, on East Falkland. In 1765 a British expedition under Commodore John Byron claimed West Falkland, and in 1766 the British established Port Egmont on Saunders Island. Spain, asserting prior rights under earlier treaties, bought out the French settlement in about 1767 and administered the islands from Buenos Aires, then part of the Spanish Empire.

Tensions between Britain and Spain nearly brought war: a Spanish force expelled the British from Port Egmont in 1770, but the outpost was restored in 1771 after a diplomatic settlement. Britain withdrew its garrison in 1774 for reasons of economy, leaving a plaque asserting its continuing claim, while Spain maintained a presence into the early 19th century. By about 1811, amid the upheavals that would dissolve Spanish rule in the Americas, the last Spanish garrison had departed, again leaving the islands without a settled administration — and with claims unresolved.

events.title: First recorded sighting
events.summary: The English navigator John Davis is credited with the first recorded European sighting of the uninhabited islands; the territory marks Falklands Day on 14 August in commemoration.

events.title: French Port Louis founded
events.summary: Louis-Antoine de Bougainville established the first settlement, Port Louis, on East Falkland, and named the islands the Malouines — origin of the Spanish Malvinas.

events.title: British Port Egmont established
events.summary: A British expedition under Commodore John Byron claimed West Falkland in 1765, and Britain founded Port Egmont on Saunders Island the following year.

events.title: Spain buys out the French and administers from Buenos Aires
events.summary: Spain purchased the French settlement around 1767 and governed the islands from Buenos Aires; in 1770 it expelled the British from Port Egmont, restored after a 1771 settlement.

pullquote.text: The Islands were entirely unoccupied until 1765, when they were first claimed by the British who established a garrison at Port Egmont.

eras.title: Buenos Aires, Vernet, and 1833
eras.standfirst: After Buenos Aires declared independence it asserted sovereignty and planted a settlement at Port Louis; a U.S. raid and then a British return in 1833 reset control — the founding of the islands' continuous administration, and the start of Argentina's enduring claim.
body: Buenos Aires declared independence from Spain in 1816 and, as heir to the Spanish viceroyalty, proclaimed its sovereignty over the Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas) in 1820. In the late 1820s the merchant Luis Vernet established a settlement at Port Louis and was given authority by Buenos Aires; he sought to regulate sealing and fishing in the surrounding waters. This is the Argentine presence on which Buenos Aires would later rest much of its case — a presence Britain regarded as inconsistent with its own claim dating from the 1760s.

The settlement was disrupted from an unexpected quarter. In 1831 the U.S. warship USS Lexington raided Port Louis in reprisal for Vernet's seizure of American sealing vessels, damaging the colony and effectively ending Vernet's enterprise. Into the resulting disorder, and asserting its long-standing claim, Britain dispatched a naval force. On 2 January 1833 it required the small Argentine garrison to depart — without bloodshed by the British and islander account — and re-established British control over the islands.

From this point a permanent British administration took shape: settlers were invited to remain, and in 1841/1842 Britain appointed a civil lieutenant governor. The United Kingdom treats 1833 as the foundation of its modern, continuous administration and bases its position on that continuity and on the wishes of the inhabitants. Argentina, by contrast, regards the events of 1833 as the unlawful expulsion of its authorities from territory it had inherited from Spain, and from that grievance dates the sovereignty claim it has pressed ever since. Both positions are set out here without endorsement.

events.title: Buenos Aires proclaims sovereignty
events.summary: Having declared independence from Spain in 1816, the Buenos Aires government proclaimed its sovereignty over the islands, the basis of Argentina's later claim.

events.title: USS Lexington raid on Port Louis
events.summary: The U.S. warship Lexington destroyed the Argentine settlement on East Falkland in reprisal for the seizure of American sealing ships, ending Vernet's colony.

events.title: Britain re-establishes control (founding of continuous administration)
events.summary: A British force required the small Argentine garrison to leave Port Louis and re-asserted British control — the start of continuous British administration, which Argentina contests as illegitimate occupation.

events.title: First British lieutenant governor
events.summary: Britain appointed a civilian lieutenant governor for the Falklands, formalising the colonial administration begun in 1833.

eras.title: The Pastoral Colony of Sheep and Wool
eras.standfirst: Around the new capital at Stanley, a sheep-farming colony took root: wool became the islands' livelihood, large estates spread across the camp, and a settled British-descended community formed.
body: In 1845 Stanley, on East Falkland, became the capital, replacing the older settlement at Port Louis, and it grew into the islands' port and administrative centre. Over the following decades the Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas) developed the pastoral economy that would define the colony for more than a century. Sheep replaced earlier wild cattle as the mainstay, and wool became the leading export, shipped to Britain from a scattering of farms and settlements across the wind-swept grassland the islanders call the camp.

The Falkland Islands Company, granted a royal charter in 1851 and incorporated the following year, became the largest landowner and sheep rancher and a central force in the colony's economy. Large estates and family-run stations spread across both main islands, their isolated settlements linked by sea and, later, by small aircraft. By the late 19th century the British-descended community had become largely self-supporting; Britannica records a population of some 1,800 by 1885, the ancestors of today's islanders.

The colony's strategic position on the route around Cape Horn gave it a wider importance. In December 1914 the waters off the islands saw the Battle of the Falkland Islands, in which a British squadron destroyed a German cruiser force early in the First World War. Through both world wars the islands served as a South Atlantic naval station, but their economic life remained that of a small, remote wool colony, its sourced figures and gaps a reminder of how thinly the archipelago was peopled.

events.title: Stanley becomes the capital
events.summary: The seat of government moved to Stanley on East Falkland, which became the islands' capital, port and main settlement.

events.title: Falkland Islands Company chartered
events.summary: The Falkland Islands Company received a royal charter in 1851 and grew into the colony's largest landowner and sheep rancher, central to its wool economy.

events.title: A self-supporting wool colony
events.summary: By 1885 a British community of some 1,800 people on the islands was self-supporting, sustained mainly by sheep farming and wool exports.

events.title: Battle of the Falkland Islands
events.summary: A British naval squadron destroyed a German cruiser force off the islands in an early First World War engagement, underscoring their strategic position near Cape Horn.

eras.title: Decolonisation and the Sovereignty Dispute
eras.standfirst: As decolonisation reshaped the post-war world, the United Nations listed the islands as a Non-Self-Governing Territory and recognised a sovereignty dispute, calling on Britain and Argentina to negotiate.
body: After the Second World War the islands entered the international decolonisation framework. The United Kingdom transmitted information on the Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas) under Article 73(e) of the UN Charter, and the territory has appeared on the UN list of Non-Self-Governing Territories since 1946. From 1964 the General Assembly's Special Committee on Decolonisation began considering 'the question of the Falkland Islands (Malvinas)', placing the territory's status squarely on the United Nations agenda.

In 1965 the General Assembly adopted Resolution 2065 (XX), which recognised the existence of a sovereignty dispute between Argentina and the United Kingdom and invited the two governments to negotiate a peaceful solution, bearing in mind the interests of the population of the islands. The resolution framed the matter as a colonial situation to be resolved by the two states — language the Decolonisation Committee has reiterated in annual resolutions ever since, without taking a position on which state holds sovereignty.

Through the 1960s and 1970s Britain and Argentina held intermittent talks. Argentina pressed its claim with growing insistence, while successive British governments stressed the wishes of the islanders, who overwhelmingly wished to remain British. Practical cooperation developed in some areas — air and shipping links, fuel supply — but the underlying sovereignty question went unresolved, and by the early 1980s relations had grown tense, the diplomatic track stalled while Argentina's military government weighed more forceful options.

events.title: Listed as a Non-Self-Governing Territory
events.summary: The Falkland Islands (Malvinas) were placed on the UN list of Non-Self-Governing Territories after the UK transmitted information under Article 73(e) of the Charter.

events.title: Decolonisation Committee takes up the question
events.summary: From 1964 the UN Special Committee on Decolonisation began considering the question of the Falkland Islands (Malvinas) and the dispute between the two governments.

events.title: General Assembly Resolution 2065 (XX)
events.summary: The UN General Assembly recognised a sovereignty dispute between Argentina and the United Kingdom and invited both to negotiate a peaceful settlement, taking account of the islanders' interests.

pullquote.text: The Falkland Islands (Malvinas) have been on the United Nations list of Non-Self-Governing Territories since 1946.
pullquote.attribution: United Nations, The United Nations and Decolonization

eras.title: The 1982 War
eras.standfirst: On 2 April 1982 Argentine forces invaded; Britain dispatched a task force across 13,000 km of ocean, and after a 74-day campaign the islands were retaken, at a cost of roughly a thousand lives on both sides.
body: On 2 April 1982 Argentine military forces invaded the Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas), quickly overwhelming the small British garrison and forcing its surrender. Argentina's ruling military junta, facing economic crisis and domestic unrest, presented the operation as the recovery of the Malvinas. Britain, under Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher, condemned the invasion as aggression against its territory and within days assembled a naval task force — built around the carriers HMS Hermes and HMS Invincible — to sail the roughly 13,000 km to the South Atlantic and retake the islands.

After failed diplomacy, including U.S. mediation by Secretary of State Alexander Haig and a later shift to support for Britain, the conflict turned to combat. On 2 May a British submarine sank the Argentine cruiser ARA General Belgrano with heavy loss of life; on 4 May an Argentine Exocet missile sank the destroyer HMS Sheffield. British troops landed at San Carlos on East Falkland on 21 May, then fought their way across the island through Goose Green and the hills around the capital, supported by naval and air forces under harsh conditions.

Argentine forces in Stanley surrendered on 14 June 1982, ending 74 days of occupation. The war cost approximately a thousand lives in all: about 255 British service personnel and some 649 Argentine personnel were killed, and three islander women died in the fighting. The defeat precipitated the fall of Argentina's junta and the return of civilian government, while Britain retained the islands. This entry treats the war soberly: the human cost fell on both sides, and the underlying sovereignty dispute was not settled by force of arms.

events.title: Argentine invasion
events.summary: Argentine forces landed and seized the islands, overwhelming the small British garrison; Argentina described it as recovering the Malvinas, Britain as an invasion of its territory.

events.title: Belgrano and Sheffield sunk
events.summary: A British submarine sank the Argentine cruiser General Belgrano on 2 May; on 4 May an Argentine Exocet missile sank the destroyer HMS Sheffield.

events.title: British landings at San Carlos
events.summary: British forces landed at San Carlos on East Falkland and advanced overland, capturing Darwin and Goose Green before closing on Stanley.

events.title: Argentine surrender at Stanley
events.summary: Argentine forces surrendered at Stanley after a 74-day campaign; about 255 British and 649 Argentine personnel and three islanders died in the war.

pullquote.text: Following a three-week ground campaign, British forces re-captured the islands' capital, Port Stanley, on June 14, forcing the surrender of all Argentine troops.
pullquote.attribution: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

eras.title: Referendum, Fisheries and Oil to the Present
eras.standfirst: Since the war the islands have prospered on fisheries and looked to offshore oil, voted overwhelmingly in 2013 to remain British, and remained the subject of an unresolved dispute the UN still asks the two governments to negotiate.
body: After 1982 the Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas) transformed economically. From 1986 the government established a fisheries conservation and licensing zone, and revenue from licensing foreign vessels to fish for squid (Illex and Loligo) became the mainstay of public finances, alongside the older sheep-and-wool sector. A 2009 constitution affirmed the islanders' right to self-determination, and the territory became internally self-governing under a British-appointed governor, with the United Kingdom responsible for defence and foreign affairs.

In a referendum held on 10–11 March 2013, islanders were asked whether they wished to remain a British Overseas Territory. On a turnout of about 92 per cent, some 99.8 per cent voted yes, with only three votes against; international observers judged the vote free and fair. The United Kingdom cited the result as a clear expression of the islanders' wishes. Argentina rejected the referendum's validity, arguing that the sovereignty dispute is a matter between states under UN resolutions and that the wishes of a transplanted population cannot determine the outcome — the two positions presented here without endorsement.

Into the 2020s the dispute remained unresolved. The UN Special Committee on Decolonisation continued to adopt annual resolutions, including in 2025, urging Argentina and the United Kingdom to resume negotiations toward a peaceful settlement, while Britain maintains there is nothing to negotiate against the islanders' wishes. Offshore exploration in the North Falkland Basin identified the Sea Lion oil discovery, whose long-delayed development advanced toward a final investment decision in the mid-2020s, holding out the prospect of a further economic transformation for a territory whose political status, more than 190 years after 1833, is still contested.

events.title: Fisheries licensing zone established
events.summary: The Falkland Islands government created a fisheries conservation and licensing zone; squid licensing revenue became the mainstay of the post-war economy.

events.title: Sovereignty referendum
events.summary: About 99.8% of islanders voting (turnout c. 92%) chose to remain a British Overseas Territory; the UK upheld the result while Argentina rejected its validity.

events.title: UN reiterates call for negotiations
events.summary: The UN Special Committee on Decolonisation again adopted a resolution asking Argentina and the United Kingdom to resume talks toward resolving the sovereignty dispute.

events.title: Contested status and an oil prospect
events.summary: The islands remain a British Overseas Territory claimed by Argentina, with an economy led by fisheries and the prospect of Sea Lion offshore oil development.

pullquote.text: We requested the Governments of Argentina and the United Kingdom to resume negotiations in order to find as soon as possible a peaceful solution to the sovereignty dispute.
pullquote.attribution: UN Special Committee on Decolonization, paraphrasing its recurring resolution

figures.name: Luis Vernet
figures.role: Merchant and Buenos Aires-appointed commander at Port Louis
figures.blurb: A Hamburg-born merchant who from the late 1820s established a settlement at Port Louis under authority from Buenos Aires and sought to regulate sealing in the islands' waters; his colony was wrecked by the 1831 USS Lexington raid, and his governorship is central to Argentina's account of its pre-1833 presence.

figures.name: Margaret Thatcher
figures.role: Prime Minister of the United Kingdom during the 1982 war
figures.blurb: British prime minister who, after Argentina's invasion on 2 April 1982, ordered the naval task force that retook the islands by 14 June; the victory bolstered her government and reaffirmed Britain's commitment to the territory.

figures.name: Leopoldo Galtieri
figures.role: President of Argentina and head of the junta in 1982
figures.blurb: Leader of Argentina's military government who ordered the 1982 occupation of the Islas Malvinas; the defeat led to his resignation days after the surrender and hastened the end of military rule and the return of civilian government.

sources.label: The South Atlantic Crisis, 1982 (Conflict over the Falkland Islands / Islas Malvinas)

sources.publisher: United Nations Meetings Coverage and Press Releases
